subject: general-knowledge
description: European History MCQs for PPSC, FPSC, NTS, CSS & PMS.

mcqs.q: The Ancient Period ended in 476 AD with the fall of the:
mcqs.explanation: The fall of the Western Roman Empire in 476 AD marks the end of the Ancient Period in world history.

mcqs.q: Athenian democracy, considered the birthplace of democratic governance, emerged around:
mcqs.explanation: Athenian democracy emerged in the 5th century BC under leaders like Pericles, establishing direct citizen participation.

mcqs.q: The Peloponnesian War (431-404 BC) weakened Greek city-states through conflict between:
mcqs.explanation: The Peloponnesian War (431-404 BC) was fought between Athens (Delian League) and Sparta (Peloponnesian League).

mcqs.q: Julius Caesar was assassinated on the Ides of March in:
mcqs.explanation: Julius Caesar was assassinated on 15 March 44 BC by a group of Roman senators including Brutus and Cassius.

mcqs.q: The Roman Republic transitioned to the Roman Empire under:
mcqs.explanation: Octavian became Augustus in 27 BC, marking the transition from the Roman Republic to the Roman Empire.

mcqs.q: The Colosseum in Rome was built during the reign of the:
mcqs.explanation: The Colosseum was built between 70-80 AD under Emperors Vespasian and Titus of the Flavian dynasty.

mcqs.q: The fall of the Western Roman Empire in 476 AD was caused by:
mcqs.explanation: The Western Roman Empire fell in 476 AD when the Germanic chieftain Odoacer deposed the last emperor Romulus Augustulus.

mcqs.q: The Byzantine Empire (Eastern Roman Empire) survived until:
mcqs.explanation: The Byzantine Empire survived until 1453 when Constantinople fell to the Ottoman Turks under Sultan Mehmed II.

mcqs.q: The Magna Carta, limiting royal power, was signed in England in:
mcqs.explanation: The Magna Carta was signed on 15 June 1215 at Runnymede, when English barons forced King John to accept limits on royal authority.

mcqs.q: The Battle of Hastings (1066) established Norman rule in England under:
mcqs.explanation: William the Conqueror defeated King Harold II at the Battle of Hastings in 1066, becoming King of England.

mcqs.q: The Black Death (bubonic plague) devastated Europe, killing roughly one-third of its population during:
mcqs.explanation: The Black Death ravaged Europe from 1347-1351, killing an estimated 25-50 million people (about one-third of Europe's population).

mcqs.q: The Hundred Years' War (1337-1453) was fought between:
mcqs.explanation: The Hundred Years' War (1337-1453) was a series of conflicts between England and France over territorial and dynastic claims.

mcqs.q: Joan of Arc rallied French forces during the Hundred Years' War and was burned at the stake in:
mcqs.explanation: Joan of Arc was captured and burned at the stake by the English in 1431. She had led French forces to key victories including the siege of Orléans.

mcqs.q: The Renaissance, a revival of classical learning, began in which country?
mcqs.explanation: The Renaissance began in Italy in the 14th century, particularly in Florence under the patronage of the Medici family.

mcqs.q: The Gutenberg printing press, which revolutionized knowledge dissemination, was invented around:
mcqs.explanation: Johannes Gutenberg invented the movable-type printing press around 1440 in Mainz, Germany, revolutionizing the spread of knowledge.

mcqs.q: Martin Luther's 95 Theses, sparking the Protestant Reformation, were posted in:
mcqs.explanation: Martin Luther posted his 95 Theses on the door of the Wittenberg Church on 31 October 1517, challenging Catholic Church practices.

mcqs.q: The Council of Trent (1545-1563) was the Catholic Church's response to the Protestant Reformation, known as the:
mcqs.explanation: The Council of Trent (1545-1563) led the Counter-Reformation, reforming Catholic practices and reaffirming doctrine.

mcqs.q: The Thirty Years' War (1618-1648), one of Europe's most destructive religious conflicts, ended with the:
mcqs.explanation: The Thirty Years' War ended with the Peace of Westphalia (1648), establishing the principle of state sovereignty in Europe.

mcqs.q: Christopher Columbus reached the Americas in:
mcqs.explanation: Christopher Columbus, sailing for Spain, reached the Americas on 12 October 1492, landing in the Bahamas.

mcqs.q: Vasco da Gama reached India by sea around the Cape of Good Hope in:
mcqs.explanation: Portuguese explorer Vasco da Gama reached Calicut, India in 1498, establishing a direct sea route from Europe to Asia.

mcqs.q: The first circumnavigation of the globe was begun by Magellan and completed in:
mcqs.explanation: Ferdinand Magellan began the first circumnavigation in 1519. After his death in the Philippines, his crew completed it in 1522.

mcqs.q: The Treaty of Tordesillas (1494) divided the New World between:
mcqs.explanation: The Treaty of Tordesillas (1494) divided newly discovered lands between Spain and Portugal along a meridian line.

mcqs.q: The French Revolution began with the storming of the Bastille on:
mcqs.explanation: The storming of the Bastille prison on 14 July 1789 marked the beginning of the French Revolution. It is celebrated as France's national day.

mcqs.q: The Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen was adopted during the French Revolution in:
mcqs.explanation: The Declaration of the Rights of Man was adopted on 26 August 1789, establishing fundamental rights and principles of the French Revolution.

mcqs.q: King Louis XVI of France was executed by guillotine in:
mcqs.explanation: King Louis XVI was executed on 21 January 1793 during the Reign of Terror phase of the French Revolution.

mcqs.q: The Reign of Terror during the French Revolution was led by:
mcqs.explanation: Robespierre led the Reign of Terror (1793-1794), during which thousands were executed by guillotine in the name of revolutionary justice.

mcqs.q: Napoleon Bonaparte crowned himself Emperor of France in:
mcqs.explanation: Napoleon crowned himself Emperor on 2 December 1804 at Notre-Dame Cathedral in Paris, in the presence of Pope Pius VII.

mcqs.q: Napoleon was decisively defeated at the Battle of Waterloo in:
mcqs.explanation: Napoleon was defeated at Waterloo (Belgium) on 18 June 1815 by British and Prussian forces under Wellington and Blücher.

mcqs.q: Napoleon's disastrous invasion of Russia, which destroyed his Grand Army, took place in:
mcqs.explanation: Napoleon invaded Russia in 1812 with over 600,000 troops. The campaign ended in catastrophic defeat with fewer than 100,000 returning.

mcqs.q: The Congress of Vienna (1814-1815) aimed to restore European order after:
mcqs.explanation: The Congress of Vienna (1814-1815) redrew European borders and restored monarchies after Napoleon's defeat.

mcqs.q: The Industrial Revolution began in which country in the late 18th century?
mcqs.explanation: The Industrial Revolution began in Britain in the 1760s, driven by innovations in textile manufacturing, steam power, and iron production.

mcqs.q: James Watt's improved steam engine, a key driver of the Industrial Revolution, was developed around:
mcqs.explanation: James Watt patented his improved steam engine in 1769, dramatically increasing the efficiency of steam power for industry.

mcqs.q: The world's first public railway using steam locomotives opened between:
mcqs.explanation: The Stockton and Darlington Railway opened in 1825, becoming the world's first public railway to use steam locomotion.

mcqs.q: The Communist Manifesto was published by Karl Marx and Friedrich Engels in:
mcqs.explanation: Marx and Engels published The Communist Manifesto in 1848, outlining their theory of class struggle and calling for workers' revolution.

mcqs.q: The unification of Italy (Risorgimento) was completed under King Victor Emmanuel II in:
mcqs.explanation: Italy was unified in 1861 with Victor Emmanuel II as king, though Rome was not incorporated until 1870.

mcqs.q: The unification of Germany was achieved under Chancellor Otto von Bismarck in:
mcqs.explanation: German unification was proclaimed on 18 January 1871 at the Palace of Versailles after Prussia's victory in the Franco-Prussian War.

mcqs.q: The Crimean War (1853-1856) saw an alliance of Britain, France, and the Ottoman Empire against:
mcqs.explanation: The Crimean War pitted an alliance of Britain, France, Sardinia, and the Ottoman Empire against Russia over influence in the declining Ottoman Empire.

mcqs.q: World War I was triggered by the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand in Sarajevo on:
mcqs.explanation: Archduke Franz Ferdinand of Austria-Hungary was assassinated on 28 June 1914 in Sarajevo by Gavrilo Princip, triggering WWI.

mcqs.q: The Allied Powers in World War I included:
options: Britain, France, Russia, and later the USA
Germany, Austria-Hungary, and the Ottoman Empire
mcqs.explanation: The Allied Powers included Britain, France, Russia (until 1917), Italy, and the USA (from 1917).

mcqs.q: The Central Powers in World War I included:
options: Germany, Austria-Hungary, Ottoman Empire, and Bulgaria
mcqs.explanation: The Central Powers were Germany, Austria-Hungary, the Ottoman Empire, and Bulgaria.

mcqs.q: The Battle of the Somme (1916), one of WWI's bloodiest battles, was fought in:
mcqs.explanation: The Battle of the Somme (July-November 1916) was fought in northern France, with over 1 million casualties on both sides.

mcqs.q: The Russian Revolution of 1917 led to the rise of:
mcqs.explanation: The October Revolution of 1917 brought the Bolsheviks under Vladimir Lenin to power, establishing Soviet communist rule.

mcqs.q: World War I ended with the Armistice signed on:
mcqs.explanation: WWI ended with the Armistice on 11 November 1918 at 11 AM (the 11th hour of the 11th day of the 11th month).

mcqs.q: The Treaty of Versailles, ending World War I, was signed on:
mcqs.explanation: The Treaty of Versailles was signed on 28 June 1919, imposing harsh terms on Germany including reparations and territorial losses.

mcqs.q: The League of Nations, the first international peacekeeping body, was established in:
mcqs.explanation: The League of Nations was established on 10 January 1920 as part of the Paris Peace Conference, though it failed to prevent WWII.

mcqs.q: World War II began when Germany invaded Poland on:
mcqs.explanation: WWII began on 1 September 1939 when Nazi Germany invaded Poland. Britain and France declared war on Germany two days later.

mcqs.q: Adolf Hitler became Chancellor of Germany in:
mcqs.explanation: Adolf Hitler was appointed Chancellor of Germany on 30 January 1933, beginning the Nazi dictatorship.

mcqs.q: The Battle of Britain (1940) was primarily an air campaign between:
mcqs.explanation: The Battle of Britain (July-October 1940) was fought in the skies over Britain between the German Luftwaffe and the Royal Air Force (RAF).

mcqs.q: D-Day, the Allied invasion of Normandy, took place on:
mcqs.explanation: D-Day (Operation Overlord) on 6 June 1944 was the largest amphibious invasion in history, landing Allied forces on the beaches of Normandy, France.

mcqs.q: Germany surrendered unconditionally, ending WWII in Europe (V-E Day), on:
mcqs.explanation: V-E Day (Victory in Europe) was celebrated on 8 May 1945 when Nazi Germany surrendered unconditionally to the Allied forces.

mcqs.q: The Holocaust was the systematic genocide of approximately six million Jews by:
mcqs.explanation: The Holocaust was the systematic murder of approximately six million European Jews by Nazi Germany and its collaborators during WWII.

mcqs.q: The Nuremberg Trials prosecuted major Nazi war criminals in:
mcqs.explanation: The Nuremberg Trials (1945-1946) were international military tribunals that tried major Nazi leaders for war crimes and crimes against humanity.

mcqs.q: The Battle of Stalingrad (1942-1943), a turning point of WWII, was fought in:
mcqs.explanation: The Battle of Stalingrad (August 1942-February 1943) was a devastating Soviet victory that turned the tide of WWII on the Eastern Front.

mcqs.q: NATO (North Atlantic Treaty Organization) was established in:
mcqs.explanation: NATO was established on 4 April 1949 as a collective defense alliance of Western nations against the Soviet threat.

mcqs.q: The Warsaw Pact, the Soviet-led military alliance, was formed in:
mcqs.explanation: The Warsaw Pact was formed on 14 May 1955 as a collective defense treaty among the Soviet Union and its Eastern Bloc allies.

mcqs.q: The Berlin Wall was built to divide East and West Berlin in:
mcqs.explanation: The Berlin Wall was built starting 13 August 1961 by East Germany to prevent its citizens from fleeing to West Berlin.

mcqs.q: The Berlin Wall fell, symbolizing the end of the Cold War, on:
mcqs.explanation: The Berlin Wall fell on 9 November 1989 as East Germany opened its borders amid peaceful protests and reformist pressures.

mcqs.q: German reunification officially took place on:
mcqs.explanation: East and West Germany were formally reunified on 3 October 1990, less than a year after the Berlin Wall fell.

mcqs.q: The Soviet Union was officially dissolved on:
mcqs.explanation: The Soviet Union was officially dissolved on 26 December 1991, ending the Cold War and creating 15 independent republics.

mcqs.q: The Iron Curtain, a term describing the Cold War division of Europe, was popularized by:
mcqs.explanation: Winston Churchill popularized the term 'Iron Curtain' in his 1946 speech at Fulton, Missouri, describing the division of Europe.

mcqs.q: The Marshall Plan (1948) provided American economic aid to rebuild:
mcqs.explanation: The Marshall Plan (European Recovery Program) provided over $13 billion in US aid to rebuild Western European economies after WWII.

mcqs.q: The Truman Doctrine (1947) committed the US to containing Soviet expansion, first applied in:
mcqs.explanation: The Truman Doctrine (1947) provided US support to Greece and Turkey to prevent them from falling under Soviet influence.

mcqs.q: The European Economic Community (EEC), predecessor to the EU, was established by the Treaty of Rome in:
mcqs.explanation: The EEC was established by the Treaty of Rome on 25 March 1957, originally with six founding members: France, West Germany, Italy, Belgium, Netherlands, and Luxembourg.

mcqs.q: The European Union was formally established by the Maastricht Treaty in:
mcqs.explanation: The EU was formally established on 1 November 1993 when the Maastricht Treaty came into force, expanding European integration beyond economics.

mcqs.q: The Euro currency was introduced for electronic transactions in:
mcqs.explanation: The Euro was introduced as an electronic currency on 1 January 1999, with physical banknotes and coins following on 1 January 2002.

mcqs.q: Brexit (UK leaving the European Union) officially took effect on:
mcqs.explanation: Brexit officially took effect on 31 January 2020, though the transition period lasted until 31 December 2020.

mcqs.q: The English Civil War (1642-1651) resulted in the execution of:
mcqs.explanation: The English Civil War ended with the execution of King Charles I in 1649. Oliver Cromwell then ruled England as Lord Protector.

mcqs.q: The Glorious Revolution of 1688 replaced King James II with:
mcqs.explanation: The Glorious Revolution of 1688 replaced the Catholic King James II with the Protestant William III and Mary II without bloodshed.

mcqs.q: The British Empire at its peak in the early 20th century controlled approximately:
options: One quarter of the world's land area
mcqs.explanation: At its peak, the British Empire controlled about 25% of the world's land area and 25% of the world's population.

mcqs.q: Queen Victoria's reign, the longest in British history at that time, lasted from:
mcqs.explanation: Queen Victoria reigned from 1837 to 1901 (63 years), presiding over the Victorian era and the height of the British Empire.

mcqs.q: The Russian Revolution of October 1917 brought which party to power?
mcqs.explanation: The October Revolution of 1917 brought the Bolsheviks under Vladimir Lenin to power, establishing the world's first communist state.

mcqs.q: The Soviet Union was formally established in:
mcqs.explanation: The Union of Soviet Socialist Republics (USSR) was formally established on 30 December 1922, uniting Russia, Ukraine, Belarus, and Transcaucasia.

mcqs.q: Joseph Stalin ruled the Soviet Union from 1924 until his death in:
mcqs.explanation: Joseph Stalin led the Soviet Union from 1924 (after Lenin's death) until his own death on 5 March 1953.

mcqs.q: The Spanish Civil War (1936-1939), often seen as a prelude to WWII, resulted in the dictatorship of:
mcqs.explanation: The Spanish Civil War ended with victory for Francisco Franco's Nationalists, establishing his dictatorship that lasted until 1975.

mcqs.q: The Enlightenment, emphasizing reason, science, and individual rights, peaked in which century?
mcqs.explanation: The Enlightenment peaked in the 18th century with thinkers like Voltaire, Rousseau, Locke, and Montesquieu challenging traditional authority.

mcqs.q: The Congress of Berlin (1878) reshaped the map of which region after the Russo-Turkish War?
mcqs.explanation: The Congress of Berlin (1878) redrew Balkan boundaries, granting independence to Romania, Serbia, and Montenegro, and autonomy to Bulgaria.

mcqs.q: The Bolshevik leader who led the Russian Revolution of 1917 was:
mcqs.explanation: Vladimir Lenin led the Bolshevik Revolution in October 1917, overthrowing the Provisional Government and establishing Soviet power.

mcqs.q: Mikhail Gorbachev's policies of Glasnost (openness) and Perestroika (restructuring) led to:
options: The collapse of the Soviet Union
mcqs.explanation: Gorbachev's reforms of Glasnost and Perestroika in the 1980s inadvertently accelerated the collapse of the Soviet Union by 1991.

mcqs.q: The Solidarity movement in Poland, led by Lech Walesa, played a key role in ending communism in:
mcqs.explanation: The Solidarity trade union, founded in 1980 by Lech Walesa, became a major force in ending communist rule in Poland and inspiring movements across Eastern Europe.

mcqs.q: The Velvet Revolution peacefully ended communist rule in Czechoslovakia in:
mcqs.explanation: The Velvet Revolution of November 1989 peacefully ended 41 years of communist rule in Czechoslovakia, with Vaclav Havel becoming president.

mcqs.q: The Yugoslav Wars of the 1990s resulted in the breakup of Yugoslavia into how many countries?
mcqs.explanation: Yugoslavia broke up into seven countries: Slovenia, Croatia, Bosnia, Serbia, Montenegro, North Macedonia, and Kosovo.

mcqs.q: The Schengen Agreement, allowing free movement across European borders, was signed in:
mcqs.explanation: The Schengen Agreement was signed on 14 June 1985, creating a zone of free movement without passport controls between member countries.
